title: Notification System — System Design
description: Designing a system that delivers notifications — push, email, SMS, in-app — reliably and at scale, without spamming users or losing messages. We build from a single synchronous send toward a queued, deduplicated, preference-aware, multi-channel fanout platform, treating reliable asynchronous delivery and user control as the core problems.

nodes.label: API
nodes.description: API: Synchronous application component that owns request-time business logic.

nodes.label: Notification API
nodes.description: Notification API: Synchronous application component that owns request-time business logic.

nodes.label: Client
nodes.description: Client: Client-side application, SDK, portal, device, or upstream caller software outside the backend boundary.

nodes.label: Client / Service
nodes.description: Client / Service: Synchronous application component that owns request-time business logic.

nodes.label: DLQ
nodes.description: DLQ: Work queue or broker abstraction that buffers tasks for consumers.

nodes.label: Notifications DB
nodes.description: Notifications DB: Authoritative operational state store for structured records or correctness-critical state.

nodes.label: Dead-Letter Queue
nodes.description: Dead-Letter Queue: Work queue or broker abstraction that buffers tasks for consumers.

nodes.label: Email Provider
nodes.description: Email Provider: Third-party or separately managed system outside the design's operational ownership boundary.

nodes.label: Fanout Service
nodes.description: Fanout Service: Synchronous application component that owns request-time business logic.

nodes.label: Dedup Store
nodes.description: Dedup Store: Authoritative operational state store for structured records or correctness-critical state.

nodes.label: Idempotency / Dedup Store
nodes.description: Idempotency / Dedup Store: Authoritative operational state store for structured records or correctness-critical state.

nodes.label: Rate Limiter / Throttler
nodes.description: Rate Limiter / Throttler: Synchronous application component that owns request-time business logic.

nodes.label: Provider
nodes.description: Provider: Third-party or separately managed system outside the design's operational ownership boundary.

nodes.label: Preferences Store
nodes.description: Preferences Store: Authoritative operational state store for structured records or correctness-critical state.

nodes.label: Preference Service
nodes.description: Preference Service: Synchronous application component that owns request-time business logic.

nodes.label: Push/Email/SMS Provider
nodes.description: Push/Email/SMS Provider: Third-party or separately managed system outside the design's operational ownership boundary.

nodes.label: APNs / FCM
nodes.description: APNs / FCM: Third-party or separately managed system outside the design's operational ownership boundary.

nodes.label: Queue
nodes.description: Queue: Work queue or broker abstraction that buffers tasks for consumers.

nodes.label: Message Queue
nodes.description: Message Queue: Work queue or broker abstraction that buffers tasks for consumers.

nodes.label: Queue Shard A
nodes.description: Queue Shard A: Work queue or broker abstraction that buffers tasks for consumers.

nodes.label: Queue Shard B
nodes.description: Queue Shard B: Work queue or broker abstraction that buffers tasks for consumers.

nodes.label: Router
nodes.description: Router: Traffic boundary component that routes, balances, admits, or gates requests.

nodes.label: Channel Router
nodes.description: Channel Router: Traffic boundary component that routes, balances, admits, or gates requests.

nodes.label: SMS Primary
nodes.description: SMS Primary: Third-party or separately managed system outside the design's operational ownership boundary.

nodes.label: SMS Secondary
nodes.description: SMS Secondary: Third-party or separately managed system outside the design's operational ownership boundary.

nodes.label: SMS Provider
nodes.description: SMS Provider: Third-party or separately managed system outside the design's operational ownership boundary.

nodes.label: Template Service
nodes.description: Template Service: Synchronous application component that owns request-time business logic.

nodes.label: Subscriptions / Topics
nodes.description: Subscriptions / Topics: Append-only event stream, feed, bus, or log used for ordering, replay, or fan-out.

nodes.label: Worker
nodes.description: Worker: Asynchronous or background compute component that processes jobs, events, batches, or scheduled work.

nodes.label: Delivery Worker
nodes.description: Delivery Worker: Asynchronous or background compute component that processes jobs, events, batches, or scheduled work.

functional: Accept a notification request from internal services (e.g. 'order shipped').
Deliver across multiple channels: push, email, SMS, in-app.
Respect per-user preferences and opt-outs per channel and category.
Fan out one event to many recipients (e.g. a broadcast or a followed topic).
Track delivery status and expose it to senders.
nonFunctional: Reliable delivery: a accepted notification is eventually delivered or explicitly failed.
At-least-once delivery with deduplication so users aren't notified twice.
Low latency for time-sensitive notifications (OTP, security alerts).
High throughput with bursty load (marketing blasts, breaking news).
Do not spam: rate-limit per user and collapse duplicates.
Graceful degradation when a downstream provider is slow or down.

capacity.label: Notification throughput
capacity.note: Marketing blasts and breaking-news fanout spike far above average.

capacity.label: Recipients per broadcast
capacity.note: One event can fan out to millions of subscribers.

capacity.label: Channels
capacity.note: Each has a different provider, latency, and failure profile.

capacity.label: Delivery latency target
capacity.note: OTP/security alerts are urgent; marketing can be minutes.

capacity.label: Dedup window
capacity.note: Retries and duplicate events must not double-notify within the window.

capacity.label: Retention of delivery status
capacity.note: For debugging, analytics, and user-facing 'delivered' state.

api.description: Enqueue a notification for one or more recipients. Idempotency-Key dedupes retries of the same logical event.
Fetch a notification's per-channel delivery status.
Set per-channel, per-category notification preferences and quiet hours.
Subscribe a user to a topic for fanout (e.g. a channel they follow).

dataModel.name: notifications
dataModel.note: One row per logical notification request, with per-channel delivery state.

dataModel.name: deliveries
dataModel.note: One row per (notification, recipient, channel) attempt; the unit workers act on.

dataModel.name: preferences
dataModel.note: Per-user, per-category, per-channel opt-in/out plus quiet hours.

dataModel.name: dedup_keys
dataModel.note: Idempotency keys for accepted events; suppresses duplicate enqueue.

patterns.name: Work queue
patterns.what: Decouple accept from deliver with a durable queue and pull-based workers.
patterns.whenToUse: When the producer is fast and bursty but downstream delivery is slow or rate-limited.

patterns.name: Fanout-on-write vs fanout-on-read
patterns.what: Materialize a copy per recipient up front (write) or resolve recipients at send time (read).
patterns.whenToUse: Choose by recipient count and fanout frequency — big topics favor fanout-on-read.

patterns.name: Idempotency key
patterns.what: Dedupe retried events so a user is notified at most once per logical event.
patterns.whenToUse: At-least-once pipelines where producers and workers both retry.

patterns.name: Dead-letter queue
patterns.what: Route messages that exhaust retries to a separate queue for inspection instead of dropping or blocking.
patterns.whenToUse: Any retrying consumer where some messages are permanently bad.

patterns.name: Preference / policy filter
patterns.what: Apply user opt-outs, categories, and quiet hours before delivery.
patterns.whenToUse: Whenever users must control what reaches them — almost always for notifications.

patterns.name: Rate limiting / throttling
patterns.what: Cap notifications per user and smooth bursts so users aren't spammed and providers aren't overwhelmed.

patterns.name: Provider abstraction / adapter
patterns.what: A uniform channel interface behind per-provider adapters with their own retry/failover.
patterns.whenToUse: Multiple interchangeable downstreams (APNs/FCM, multiple email/SMS vendors).

patterns.name: Sharded queues
patterns.what: Partition the queue so throughput scales and one hot tenant can't starve others.
patterns.whenToUse: When a single queue/consumer group becomes the throughput bottleneck.

steps.title: 1. Synchronous Single-Channel Send (baseline)
steps.description: Start minimal: a service calls the notification API, which calls a provider (say push) synchronously and returns the result. This surfaces the core tension — delivery depends on a slow, flaky third party — before we add machinery.
steps.decisionPrompt: What happens to the caller when the push provider is slow, down, or rate-limiting you?

concepts.definition: An external delivery service (APNs/FCM for push, an email/SMS vendor).
concepts.whyItMatters: You don't control its latency or uptime, so synchronous coupling is fragile.

concepts.definition: 'Sent' means handed to the provider; 'delivered' means it reached the device — often confirmed later.
concepts.whyItMatters: Status is inherently asynchronous, which pushes the design toward queues.

talkingPoints: State the read/write profile and that bursty fanout is the real load, not steady traffic.
Point out the caller is blocked on a third party — unacceptable at 50k/s.

traps.trap: Calling the provider synchronously on the request path
traps.why: A slow or down provider blocks callers, exhausts threads, and drops notifications under load.
traps.instead: Accept fast, enqueue, and deliver asynchronously with retries.

bottlenecks.issue: Provider latency and outages propagate straight back to callers.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Introduce a queue and workers (next step).

strong: Names provider flakiness as the dominant failure mode.
weak: Assumes providers are fast and reliable.
Blocks the caller until delivery confirms.

whyNow: A concrete baseline makes each later addition a response to a real failure.

view.caption: The client service calls the notification API synchronously, which delivers to the provider and records status in the notifications DB.

steps.title: 2. Queue + Workers (reliable async delivery)
steps.description: Put a durable queue between accept and deliver. The API enqueues and returns 202 immediately; pull-based workers deliver with retries and backoff. Permanently failing messages go to a dead-letter queue rather than blocking or vanishing.
steps.decisionPrompt: How do workers retry without amplifying load during a provider outage, and where do poison messages go?

concepts.definition: The queue may redeliver a message, so a notification can be processed more than once.
concepts.whyItMatters: It's the reliable default, but forces deduplication downstream.
Prevents synchronized retry storms that hammer a recovering provider.

concepts.definition: A side queue for messages that exhaust retries.
concepts.whyItMatters: Keeps poison messages from blocking the main queue while preserving them for inspection.

options.name: Pull-based workers from a durable queue (default)
options.description: Accepted notifications land in a durable queue, and worker pools pull from it at their own pace to call providers, with retries and a DLQ on the queue.
pros: Absorbs bursts; workers consume at their own pace.
Natural backpressure — the queue grows instead of dropping.
Retries and DLQ are straightforward.
cons: Adds a queue to operate and monitor (lag, depth).

view.caption: The API enqueues onto a durable message queue from which delivery workers pull at their own pace.

options.name: Push-based fan to workers (no queue)
options.description: The API pushes each accepted notification straight to a worker for immediate delivery, with no buffering queue between them.
pros: Lower latency in the happy path.
cons: No buffer — bursts overload workers and providers.
Backpressure and retries become ad-hoc and lossy.

view.caption: The API pushes each notification directly to a delivery worker with no queue between them.

flows.name: Deliver with retry, then DLQ
flows.note: Transient failures retry with backoff; permanent ones dead-letter.

traps.why: Permanently bad messages (invalid token) retry endlessly, wasting capacity and blocking the queue.
traps.instead: Cap retries; route exhausted messages to a DLQ and stop.

traps.why: All workers retry in lockstep and hammer the recovering provider.

failureDrills.scenario: The push provider is down for 10 minutes.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: No notifications lost; delivery resumes when it recovers.
failureDrills.mitigation: Messages stay queued and retry with backoff; queue depth alerts ops; nothing is dropped.

strong: Adds backoff+jitter and a DLQ without being asked.
Treats the queue as the buffer that gives backpressure.
weak: Drops messages when workers fall behind.

recap.after: Accept is fast; delivery is reliable, retried, and buffered.

whyNow: Reliable async delivery is the backbone everything else builds on.

view.caption: The API accepts the request, returns 202, and enqueues it; a delivery worker pulls from the queue, calls the provider, records status in the DB, and dead-letters on give-up.

steps.title: 3. Idempotency & Deduplication (notify once)
steps.description: At-least-once queues and retrying producers mean the same logical event can arrive twice. An idempotency key on accept and a dedup check on deliver ensure a user is notified at most once per event within a window.
steps.decisionPrompt: Where do you dedupe — at accept, at deliver, or both — and what's the dedup window?

concepts.definition: A client-supplied key identifying one logical event so retries are recognized.
concepts.whyItMatters: Lets producers safely retry without creating duplicate notifications.

concepts.definition: The time span over which identical (user, event) pairs are collapsed.
concepts.whyItMatters: Bounds dedup storage and defines what 'duplicate' means operationally.

flows.name: Duplicate event is suppressed
flows.note: Same key at accept short-circuits before enqueue.

traps.trap: Deduping only at accept, not at delivery
traps.why: A worker retry after a crash can still deliver twice even if accept was deduped.
traps.instead: Dedupe the delivery attempt too, keyed by (delivery_id) or (user, event, channel).

traps.why: A producer retry gets a fresh key and the event is treated as new.
traps.instead: The producer supplies a stable key per logical event.

failureDrills.scenario: A producer retries POST after a timeout it didn't see succeed.
failureDrills.mitigation: Accept dedupes by key and returns the original notification_id; delivery dedupes by delivery_id.

strong: Dedupes at both accept and deliver.
Defines an explicit dedup window and its storage cost.
weak: Assumes the queue delivers exactly once.

recap.before: Retries could notify a user multiple times.
recap.after: At-most-once effect per logical event within the window.
recap.newRisk: Dedup store sizing and window choice become real decisions.

whyNow: Before fanning out to millions, duplicates must be controlled or they multiply.

view.caption: The client sends an idempotency key; the API claims it in the dedup store before enqueuing, and the worker re-checks the dedup store before delivering to the provider.

options.name: Dedupe at accept and at deliver (default)
options.description: An idempotency key rejects duplicate submissions at the API, and a second dedup check at delivery time catches at-least-once queue redelivery.
pros: An idempotency key on accept rejects duplicate producer submissions before they enter the queue.
A deliver-time dedup check catches at-least-once redelivery from the queue, so the user is notified at most once.
Two guards cover both sources of duplicates (retrying producer and redelivering queue).
cons: Two dedup checks add latency and a shared dedup store on both the accept and deliver paths.
The dedup window must be chosen carefully — too short re-notifies, too long bloats the store.
Dedup store becomes a hot dependency that must scale with traffic.

view.caption: The API claims the idempotency key in the dedup store at accept, and the worker checks it again before delivery, so duplicates are dropped at both stages.

options.name: Dedupe at accept only
options.description: A single idempotency check at the API rejects duplicate submissions, after which the queue and workers run with no further dedup.
pros: Single dedup point on the API; the rest of the pipeline stays simple.
Stops duplicate producer submissions and retried sends cheaply at the edge.
Lower latency than checking again on every delivery.
cons: At-least-once queues can still redeliver a message the worker already sent — duplicate notification.
A worker crash after sending but before acking re-sends with no second guard.
Only safe if delivery itself is naturally idempotent, which providers are not.

view.caption: The API claims the idempotency key in the dedup store before enqueuing, but the worker delivers to the provider without a second check.

options.name: Dedupe at deliver only
options.description: A single dedup check guards the provider call, dropping duplicates just before the user is notified while letting them flow through the queue.
pros: Guards the one place a duplicate actually reaches the user — the provider call.
Catches both producer retries and queue redelivery with a single check.
No accept-path coordination needed; producers can be dumb.
cons: Duplicate events still flow through the queue and consume worker capacity before being dropped.
The dedup check sits on the latency-sensitive delivery path.
A bursty duplicate producer can flood the queue even though nothing is sent twice.

view.caption: The API enqueues without dedup, and only the worker checks the dedup store before delivering to the provider.

steps.title: 4. Fanout (one event, many recipients)
steps.description: Broadcasts and topic notifications target many users. A fanout service expands one event into per-recipient deliveries. Choose fanout-on-write (materialize per recipient now) vs fanout-on-read (resolve subscribers at send time) by scale.
steps.decisionPrompt: A topic has 10M subscribers and a creator posts hourly. Do you fan out on write or on read?

concepts.definition: Create a delivery per recipient when the event arrives.
concepts.whyItMatters: Fast reads/sends but expensive for huge audiences and frequent events.

concepts.definition: Store the event once; resolve recipients lazily at delivery time.
concepts.whyItMatters: Cheap for huge, rarely-active audiences; shifts work to send time.

options.name: Hybrid: write for small audiences, read for large (default)
options.description: Small audiences get a delivery written per recipient up front, while large topics enqueue once and expand on read at delivery time.
pros: Cheap sends for huge topics; fast for small ones.
Avoids materializing 10M rows per post.
cons: Two code paths; a threshold to tune.

view.caption: The fanout service materializes per-recipient messages onto the queue for small audiences but resolves large audiences from the topics store at send time.

options.name: Always fanout-on-write
options.description: Every event materializes one delivery row per recipient at publish time, so delivery is just draining the resulting queue.
pros: Simple, uniform; delivery is just draining a queue.
cons: A 10M-subscriber post writes 10M deliveries up front — expensive and bursty.

view.caption: The fanout service materializes one message per recipient onto the queue for every broadcast.

traps.trap: Fanning out on write for celebrity-scale topics
traps.why: One post materializes millions of rows in a burst, overloading the queue and DB.
traps.instead: Use fanout-on-read (or hybrid) above a subscriber threshold.

traps.trap: Expanding subscribers synchronously in the API
traps.why: Resolving millions of recipients blocks the request.
traps.instead: Hand off to an async fanout worker that streams recipients into the queue.

failureDrills.scenario: Fanout worker crashes halfway through a 10M expansion.
failureDrills.mitigation: Checkpoint progress (cursor) and dedupe per (notification, user) so resume is idempotent.

strong: Picks the fanout strategy by audience size and frequency.
weak: Uses one fanout strategy for all cases.
Expands recipients on the request path.

recap.after: One event expands to per-recipient deliveries at any scale.
recap.newRisk: Huge fanouts are bursty; expansion must be async, checkpointed, and idempotent.

whyNow: Fanout is the load multiplier; everything downstream must tolerate its bursts.

view.caption: The API hands a broadcast to the fanout service, which expands subscribers from the topics store and enqueues one per-recipient message for workers to deliver.

flows.name: One event fans out to many recipients
flows.note: The fanout service expands a topic event into per-recipient deliveries and enqueues each; the API returns immediately.

steps.title: 5. User Preferences & Quiet Hours
steps.description: Insert a preference/policy filter before delivery: per-channel, per-category opt-outs, quiet hours, and channel fallback. Suppressed deliveries are recorded as 'suppressed', not silently dropped.
steps.decisionPrompt: Where in the pipeline do you apply preferences, and how do you keep that check fast at 50k/s?

concepts.definition: A per-user window during which non-urgent notifications are held or dropped.
concepts.whyItMatters: Respecting them is the difference between useful and spammy.

concepts.definition: Users opt out of a category (marketing) and/or a channel (SMS) independently.
concepts.whyItMatters: Real consent is multi-dimensional, not a single on/off switch.

deepDives.title: Keeping preference checks fast
points: Cache preferences in the worker (read-through) with short TTL; opt-outs are read far more than written.
Always honor urgent/transactional categories (OTP, security) regardless of marketing opt-outs.
Record suppression with a reason so senders can see 'why didn't my notification arrive'.

traps.trap: Checking preferences only at accept time
traps.why: Preferences can change between accept and delivery (often minutes later for big fanouts).
traps.instead: Apply the policy filter at delivery time, close to the send.

traps.trap: Treating quiet hours as a hard drop for everything
traps.why: Security alerts and OTPs must pierce quiet hours.
traps.instead: Classify by category; urgent categories bypass quiet hours.

failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Fail safe — don't spam, don't lose.
failureDrills.mitigation: For non-urgent categories, hold/retry; for urgent, fall back to a conservative default (deliver) and log.

strong: Applies preferences at delivery time and caches reads.
Separates urgent categories from quiet hours.

recap.after: Users control channels, categories, and timing; urgent always gets through.
recap.newRisk: The preference check is now on the hot path — must be cached and fail-safe.

whyNow: Not spamming users is a core requirement, applied right before sending.

view.caption: The worker checks the preference service (backed by the preferences store) before routing allowed notifications to the provider via the channel router, recording suppressed ones in the DB.

options.name: Preference service before delivery (default)
options.description: A dedicated preference service is called on the delivery path to apply opt-outs, quiet hours, and fallback, recording suppressed sends rather than dropping them.
pros: A dedicated preference/policy service applies per-channel and per-category opt-outs, quiet hours, and fallback in one place.
Suppressed deliveries are recorded as 'suppressed', not silently dropped, for auditability.
Centralized policy is easy to evolve without touching every producer.
cons: A synchronous policy lookup sits on the delivery path and must stay fast at 50k/s.
The preference store/service is a new dependency that must be highly available.
Hot reads require caching preferences to hit the latency budget.

view.caption: The worker queries the dedicated preference service and its store before routing allowed messages to the provider, logging suppressed ones to the DB.

options.name: Inline preference check in the worker
options.description: Each worker reads a cached preferences view and applies opt-out and quiet-hours logic itself, with no separate preference service hop.
pros: No extra service hop: the worker reads a cached preferences view and decides locally.
Lowest latency on the hot delivery path.
Fewer moving parts to operate.
cons: Every worker must embed and update policy logic — duplicated and easy to drift.
A locally cached preferences view can be stale, sending to an opted-out user.
Cross-cutting policy changes require redeploying workers.

view.caption: The worker does a cached preferences lookup itself, delivering to the provider if allowed and recording suppressed ones in the DB.

options.name: Filter at accept time
options.description: Preferences are resolved at the API before the event enters the queue, so opted-out notifications are filtered out before fanout multiplies them.
pros: Opted-out notifications never enter the queue, saving downstream capacity.
The decision is made once at the edge, before fanout multiplies the event.
Keeps delivery workers simple and policy-free.
cons: Quiet hours computed at accept can be wrong by deliver time for delayed/queued sends.
Per-recipient preferences must be resolved before fanout, which is expensive for broadcasts.
Late preference changes (user opts out after enqueue) aren't respected.

view.caption: The API checks the preference service and store at accept time and only enqueues allowed notifications for workers.

steps.title: 6. Multi-Channel Routing & Providers
steps.description: Generalize delivery across channels via a router and per-provider adapters. Each channel (push/email/SMS) has its own provider, retry profile, and failover. Templates render channel-appropriate content.
steps.decisionPrompt: How do you add a second SMS vendor for failover without touching the rest of the pipeline?

concepts.definition: A uniform interface implemented per provider (APNs, FCM, SendGrid, Twilio).
concepts.whyItMatters: Lets you add/replace/fail over providers without changing workers.

concepts.definition: Routing to a secondary provider when the primary fails or degrades.
concepts.whyItMatters: A single vendor outage shouldn't stop a whole channel.

flows.name: SMS provider failover
flows.note: Primary degrades; router fails over to secondary.

traps.trap: Hardcoding one provider per channel into the worker
traps.why: Adding failover or swapping vendors means rewriting workers and risking regressions.
traps.instead: Put providers behind a uniform adapter interface the router selects at runtime.

traps.trap: Rendering content once for all channels
traps.why: An SMS isn't an email isn't a push payload (length, format, links).
traps.instead: Render per channel via templates with channel-specific variants.

failureDrills.scenario: Primary email vendor has elevated bounce rates.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Traffic shifts to a healthy vendor without losing messages.
failureDrills.mitigation: Health-aware routing with failover; bounces feed back into suppression lists.

strong: Abstracts providers behind adapters and routes by health.
weak: Couples workers to a specific vendor.

recap.after: Many channels, swappable providers with failover and per-channel templates.
recap.newRisk: More providers means more health signals and suppression handling to manage.

whyNow: Multi-channel is a core requirement; doing it cleanly needs an abstraction, not if/else per vendor.

view.caption: The worker renders via the template service and hands the message to the channel router, which dispatches to APNs/FCM, email, or SMS providers and dead-letters exhausted ones.

options.name: Router + per-provider adapters (default)
options.description: A channel router fronts pluggable per-provider adapters, dispatching each notification to the right vendor behind one delivery interface with per-channel retry profiles.
pros: A channel router fronts pluggable adapters, so adding a vendor is a new adapter, not a pipeline rewrite.
Each channel keeps its own retry profile and failover independently.
Templates render channel-appropriate content behind one delivery interface.
cons: The router plus adapter set is more abstraction than a single hard-coded provider call.
A shared router is a common path whose failure affects every channel.
Per-channel config (retries, rate, templates) multiplies operational surface.

view.caption: The worker templates the message and the channel router fans it to per-provider adapters for push, email, and SMS, dead-lettering exhausted deliveries.

options.name: Multi-vendor failover per channel
options.description: Each channel routes to a primary vendor and falls over to a secondary on errors or rate limits, all configured behind the same router.
pros: Each channel routes to a primary vendor and fails over to a secondary on errors or rate limits.
A single vendor outage degrades gracefully instead of dropping that channel.
Adding the second SMS vendor is config behind the same router — no pipeline change.
cons: Vendors differ in features and delivery semantics, complicating the adapter abstraction.
Failover must avoid double-sending the same notification across vendors.
More vendor relationships, credentials, and health checks to operate.

view.caption: The channel router sends SMS to a primary provider and fails over to a secondary on error, dead-lettering once both are exhausted.

options.name: Single multi-channel provider
options.description: All channels are handled through one external delivery platform via a single adapter, offloading push/email/SMS specifics to the provider.
pros: Offload channel handling to one provider (e.g. a delivery platform) with a single integration.
Least code to build and operate — one adapter, one contract.
Provider handles push/email/SMS specifics and some failover for you.
cons: Hard vendor lock-in; their outage takes down all channels at once.
Less control over per-channel retry, failover, and cost optimization.
Adding a custom or cheaper vendor for one channel is no longer simple.

view.caption: The worker templates the message and hands it to one multi-channel provider for any channel, dead-lettering exhausted deliveries.

steps.title: 7. Rate Limiting & Throttling
steps.description: Cap notifications per user (don't spam) and smooth bursts toward providers (respect their limits). Combine per-user rate limits, digest/collapse for chatty categories, and provider-side throttling.
steps.decisionPrompt: How do you stop a buggy producer from notifying one user 500 times in a minute?

concepts.definition: A per-key limiter that refills at a steady rate and allows bursts up to a cap.
concepts.whyItMatters: Bounds per-user volume while tolerating short bursts.

concepts.definition: Coalescing many notifications into one summary.
concepts.whyItMatters: Turns spam into a useful roll-up for chatty categories.

options.name: Per-user token bucket + provider throttle (default)
options.description: A per-user token bucket in a shared store caps how often each recipient is messaged, layered with a provider-quota throttle on outbound sends.
pros: Caps per-user spam and respects provider quotas.
Bursts allowed up to a cap, then smoothed.
cons: Distributed counters need a fast shared store (e.g. Redis).

view.caption: The worker consults a per-user token-bucket limiter, which only lets under-limit messages through to the channel router.

options.name: Global fixed-rate throttle only
options.description: A single global rate cap limits total outbound sends, with no per-user accounting.
pros: Trivial to implement.
cons: Doesn't stop spamming a single user.
Either too loose globally or too strict for everyone.

view.caption: The worker pushes messages to the channel router at a single fixed global rate.

traps.trap: Only limiting globally, not per user
traps.why: A bug or abusive sender can still bombard one user while staying under the global cap.
traps.instead: Limit per user (and per category), plus a global/provider throttle.

traps.why: Users miss things and senders can't tell why.
traps.instead: Collapse into a digest or defer, and record the decision.

failureDrills.scenario: A producer bug emits the same alert 500x/min for one user.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: The user sees at most a small, bounded number (or a digest).
failureDrills.mitigation: Per-user token bucket caps delivery; excess is collapsed and the anomaly is alerted.

strong: Limits per user and per provider, not just globally.
Collapses excess into digests instead of dropping.
weak: Relies on a single global rate.

recap.before: A bad producer could spam a user unboundedly.
recap.after: Per-user and per-provider limits with digest fallback.
recap.newRisk: Limiter state is shared and hot — needs a fast, available counter store.

whyNow: With multi-channel fanout live, abuse and provider-limit breaches are the next real risk.

view.caption: The worker checks the per-user rate limiter before routing allowed messages to the provider via the router, recording collapsed or suppressed ones in the DB.

steps.title: 8. Scaling the Pipeline
steps.description: Shard the queue by tenant/recipient so throughput scales and one noisy tenant can't starve others. Autoscale workers on queue depth, and keep the dedup/limiter stores partitioned and fast.
steps.decisionPrompt: A single tenant's marketing blast is starving everyone else's notifications. What do you change?

concepts.definition: Splitting the queue by a key (tenant, user) across partitions/consumer groups.
concepts.whyItMatters: Scales throughput and isolates noisy tenants.

concepts.definition: Allocating delivery capacity across tenants so one can't monopolize it.
concepts.whyItMatters: Prevents one blast from delaying everyone's urgent notifications.

options.name: Per-tenant partitions + priority lanes (default)
options.description: Traffic is split into per-tenant queue partitions with separate priority lanes, so urgent notifications bypass bulk blasts and noisy tenants stay isolated.
pros: Isolates noisy tenants; urgent lane bypasses bulk blasts.
Scales horizontally by adding partitions.
cons: More partitions to manage; needs autoscaling on depth.

view.caption: Bulk traffic flows through queue shard A and urgent traffic through shard B, both drained by the same worker pool.

options.name: Single queue, scale workers only
options.description: All notifications share one queue and throughput is grown only by adding workers, with no tenant or priority partitioning.
pros: Simplest; no partitioning logic.
cons: One huge blast still head-of-line-blocks everyone.
No tenant isolation.

view.caption: A single queue shard feeds the delivery workers, which are scaled out without partitioning.

deepDives.title: Priority and fairness
points: Separate urgent (OTP/security) from bulk (marketing) into different lanes.
Within bulk, round-robin or weight by tenant so no one starves others.
Autoscale worker count on queue depth and age of the oldest message.

traps.trap: One global queue for urgent and bulk together
traps.why: A marketing blast head-of-line-blocks time-sensitive OTPs.
traps.instead: Separate priority lanes; urgent never waits behind bulk.

traps.trap: Sharding by a key that doesn't isolate tenants
traps.why: A noisy tenant still spreads across all partitions and starves others.
traps.instead: Partition by tenant (and add fair scheduling) so blast radius is contained.

strong: Separates urgent vs bulk lanes and isolates tenants.
Autoscales on queue depth/age, not CPU alone.

recap.before: One queue capped throughput and let tenants interfere.
recap.after: Sharded, prioritized, autoscaled delivery with tenant isolation.
recap.newRisk: More partitions and lanes to operate; routing keys must be chosen well.

whyNow: At 50k/s with bursty fanout, a single queue is the final bottleneck and fairness risk.

view.caption: The API partitions traffic by tenant across queue shards A and B, each drained by delivery workers that call the provider.

finalDesign.title: Final Design
finalDesign.description: End-to-end: a fast accept API with idempotency, a sharded durable queue with priority lanes, async fanout (hybrid write/read), preference-filtered delivery workers, multi-provider channel routing with failover and templates, per-user rate limiting with digests, DLQ for poison messages, and delivery tracking.

view.caption: The client calls the API, which dedupes and fans out via the topics store onto per-tenant queue shards; workers apply preferences, rate limits, and templates, then route to push/email/SMS providers (dead-lettering failures) and record status in the DB.

functional.how: Fast 202 accept API that enqueues for async delivery.
Channel router with per-provider adapters and templates.
Preference/policy filter applied at delivery time with caching.

functional.requirement: Fan out one event to many recipients
functional.how: Async fanout service, hybrid write/read by audience size.
Per-(notification, recipient, channel) delivery rows updated by workers.

nonFunctional.how: Durable queue, retries with backoff, DLQ for poison messages.
Idempotency at accept and dedup at delivery within a window.
Priority lanes keep OTP/security ahead of bulk blasts.
Sharded queues and autoscaled workers absorb fanout bursts.
Per-user rate limits and digest collapse.
Health-aware routing with failover; backoff and DLQ.

say: Restate it: deliver multi-channel notifications reliably, at scale, without spamming.
List channels, fanout, preferences as functional; reliability, dedup, no-spam, urgent-latency as non-functional.
Call out the headline tension: a slow third-party provider plus huge bursty fanout.
Size peak notifications/s, max recipients per broadcast, and per-channel latency targets.
Sketch POST /notifications (with Idempotency-Key), GET status, preferences, topic subscribe.
Start synchronous, then add the queue+workers backbone with retries and a DLQ.
Layer idempotency/dedup, then fanout, preferences, multi-channel routing, and rate limiting.
Go deep where pushed: fanout strategy, dedup, or fairness/priority lanes.
Shard queues by tenant with priority lanes; autoscale on depth.
Summarize how each requirement is met and name the top bottleneck (provider limits / fanout bursts).

expectations: Puts a queue between accept and delivery and explains why.
Knows retries need backoff and that failures shouldn't be dropped.
Designs dedup at accept and delivery; defines a dedup window.
Chooses fanout-on-write vs read by audience size; keeps expansion async.
Applies preferences at delivery with caching and urgent-category bypass.
Isolates tenants with sharded queues and fair scheduling; separates urgent vs bulk lanes.
Designs provider failover and health-aware routing across vendors.
Reasons about blast radius, backpressure, and the cost of each delivery guarantee.

followUps: How do you handle delivery receipts and read-tracking that arrive asynchronously from providers?
How would you implement scheduled and recurring notifications (send at 9am local time)?
How do you manage device-token lifecycle and prune invalid tokens fed back from providers?
How would you add an in-app inbox that must stay consistent with push delivery?
How do you A/B test notification content and measure engagement without double-counting?
How would you enforce global compliance (unsubscribe, GDPR deletion) across all channels and history?
